title: Leave a twenty in your wallet for a week
subtitle: A seven-day activity practice: same twenty-pound note, untouched, sitting in the wallet.
excerpt: A seven-day activity practice. A single twenty-pound note is placed in the wallet at the start of the week and stays there, untouched, for seven days. The activity rehearses the belief that money can sit with you without disappearing. Five minutes to set up; the practice itself runs in the background.
sourceNotes: The held-money activity is a folk-money practice with a long lineage in money-mindset work and folk-prosperity traditions. The seven-day form here is a standard pattern. Theme adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "End the feast/famine cycle".

content.text: A seven-day activity practice. One banknote, twenty pounds where the week can spare it (smaller where it can't), is placed in the wallet at the start of the week and stays there, untouched, for seven days. The note is the object; the wallet is the place; the week is the time. The body learns that money can sit with you without disappearing.
New to activity practices? Read
activities as practice
first.
What you'll need

One note in cash. A twenty-pound note is the standard size; a fiver or a tenner is fine for a week where a twenty is too much. The note has to be money that is truly spare for the week, not the food shop, not a bill that is due. The practice doesn't work with money that the household needs.
The practice
Set up, day one

Get the note. Hold it in both hands for a slow minute. Notice what comes up: the urge to spend it before it disappears, the urge to put it in a savings account where it's "safer", the urge to give it away.
Fold the note in half and place it in your wallet. Behind the cards, or in the part of the wallet where it'll stay out of immediate sight. Close the wallet.
Set a marker for seven days from now: a phone note, a date in the calendar, a sticky note on the fridge. Day seven is when the practice closes.
Through the week
Carry the wallet as usual. The note stays where you put it. Don't reorganise the wallet around it. Don't tell people about it. It isn't theatre, it's a private rehearsal.

When the urge to spend the note arrives (it usually does, somewhere between day two and day four), notice it. Note that the urge is here. Don't fight it; don't act on it. The urge is the old pattern asking for its job back.

If you absolutely have to spend the note for a real reason (an unplanned bill, a household need), spend it and restart the practice the next day with a fresh note. Don't let an unplanned cost end the practice; just restart the clock.
Close, day seven
On day seven, take the note out. Hold it for a slow minute again. Notice what's different about how it feels now versus how it felt on day one.
The note can now be spent on whatever you choose. The practice is closed. If the week of holding shifted something, run it again next month with a larger note, or whatever size the body is ready for.
Where this practice comes from

The held-money activity is a folk-money practice. It has a long lineage in money-mindset work and folk-prosperity traditions. The idea is that the body needs to feel money sit with it before the nervous system trusts that more is coming. The seven-day form used here is a standard pattern. The theme is adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program by Rebecca J Page, which uses object-based money practices alongside the tapping work.
